mcqs.question: In the Chandy-Lamport snapshot algorithm, when a process receives a marker message for the first time, it:

options.text: Records its own state and starts recording incoming channel state
Ignores the marker and continues normal computation
Immediately terminates the snapshot process
Forwards the marker without recording anything

mcqs.explanation: In Chandy-Lamport, when a process receives its first marker on a channel, it records its local state and then starts recording messages arriving on that channel until it receives a marker on all incoming channels. This ensures capture of in-transit messages.

mcqs.question: A global state is consistent if and only if:

options.text: All processes have recorded their states at exactly the same time
For every message received, the corresponding send event is included in the state
All messages in transit are captured
The system clock is synchronized across all processes

mcqs.explanation: A consistent global state satisfies the safety property: if a receive event is included in the state, the corresponding send event must also be included. This ensures no violation of causality.

mcqs.question: In the space-time diagram of a distributed computation, a cut is consistent if:

options.text: It passes through exactly one event in each process
No message crosses the cut from future to past
All messages are delivered before the cut
The cut is a vertical line in the diagram

mcqs.explanation: A consistent cut does not violate causality. In the space-time diagram, this means no message can go 'backward' in time across the cut. If a message crosses from future to past, it would create an inconsistent state where receive appears without send.

mcqs.question: The Chandy-Lamport snapshot algorithm requires:

options.text: A centralized coordinator
Communication channels to be FIFO-ordered
All processes to be idle during snapshot
Knowledge of total number of processes beforehand

mcqs.explanation: Chandy-Lamport assumes FIFO channels - messages sent by a process arrive in the order they were sent. This property is essential for correctly capturing channel states. The algorithm is distributed and does not require a coordinator.

mcqs.question: What information does a process include in its local snapshot?

options.text: Only the values of all local variables
Only the program counter position
The local state (variables, program counter, and channel queue)
The state of all other processes in the system

mcqs.explanation: A local snapshot includes the process's local state - all variables, program counter, and any messages already queued on incoming channels but not yet processed.

mcqs.question: In distributed termination detection, a process is said to be terminated when:

options.text: It has executed its last instruction
It is passive and all channels are empty
It has sent all its messages
It has received acknowledgment from all processes

mcqs.explanation: Termination detection requires both (a) each process has terminated locally (no active instructions), and (b) all channels are empty (no messages in transit). This ensures the entire computation has truly ended.

mcqs.question: What is the minimum number of markers sent by each process in Chandy-Lamport?

options.text: One marker per outgoing channel
Exactly one marker total
One marker per incoming channel
No markers - only processes send snapshots

mcqs.explanation: Each process sends exactly one marker on each of its outgoing channels. These markers propagate through the system, triggering state recording at other processes. This ensures the snapshot propagates to all reachable parts of the system.

mcqs.question: The happens-before relation (→) is:

options.text: A total order of all events
A partial order that captures causal relationships
Equivalent to real-time ordering
Only defined for messages between processes

mcqs.explanation: The happens-before relation is a partial order (not total) that captures causal relationships. Two events may be incomparable (concurrent) if neither precedes the other. It is the foundation for reasoning about consistency in distributed systems.

mcqs.question: In the context of global state detection, 'in-transit' messages are:

options.text: Messages that have been lost in transmission
Messages that have been sent but not yet received
Messages that have been received but not processed
Duplicate messages sent multiple times

mcqs.explanation: In-transit messages are those that have been sent from a source process but have not yet been received by the destination process. These messages are crucial for reconstructing the complete global state and are captured through the marker mechanism.

mcqs.question: Which of the following is NOT a property of a consistent global state?

options.text: If receive(e) is in the state, then send(e) must be in the state
The state is reachable from the initial state of the computation
All processes must record their state at exactly the same physical time
The state represents a cut that respects causality

mcqs.explanation: Consistent global states do NOT require recording at the same physical time. This is impossible due to clock drift and absence of global clock. The key is logical consistency, not temporal simultaneity.

mcqs.question: What problem can occur if we take independent snapshots of each process without coordination?

options.text: The snapshot will take too long to complete
We may capture an inconsistent global state
The processes will deadlock
Memory overflow will occur

mcqs.explanation: Independent uncoordinated snapshots can capture states at different logical times, potentially creating an inconsistent global state. For example, a receive event might be captured without its matching send event, violating consistency.

mcqs.question: For deadlock detection in distributed systems, the global state must be examined to find:

options.text: Processes that have sent the most messages
Circular wait conditions across processes
The oldest running process
Processes with highest CPU usage

mcqs.explanation: Deadlock detection requires finding a cycle in the wait-for graph. Since the graph is distributed across processes, we need a consistent global state to correctly identify cycles that represent circular waiting.

mcqs.question: What is the role of markers in Chandy-Lamport algorithm?

options.text: Markers are used to synchronize physical clocks
Markers delineate the beginning of channel state recording
Markers carry the local state to the initiator
Markers terminate the snapshot process

mcqs.explanation: Markers serve as delimiters - when a process receives a marker on a channel, it marks the boundary between messages that were 'in transit' at snapshot time (recorded) and messages that arrived after (not recorded).

mcqs.question: A global state S is 'possible' if:

options.text: It can be observed from a single monitor process
There exists some execution of the system that passes through S
All processes agree on the state
It is the initial state of the system

mcqs.explanation: A possible global state is one that could occur during some actual execution of the distributed system. Not all consistent states are possible - only those that can be reached from the initial state through valid transitions.

mcqs.question: The Lai-Yang snapshot algorithm differs from Chandy-Lamport in that:

options.text: It requires a centralized coordinator
Processes begin recording channel states from the start
It works only for synchronous systems
It cannot handle message losses

mcqs.explanation: In Lai-Yang, each process starts recording outgoing messages from the beginning of its execution (white messages), and upon receiving a marker, switches to recording only messages after the marker (gray messages). This allows full concurrent recording without waiting for markers.
